To accompany the one who departs, without binding them, to let them go with tenderness rather than hold them with anguish, is perhaps the hardest gesture of this face, and the most loving, because it is loving even at the point where love has to accept the very separation it most feared.

And in faith, the grip appears in a form that many religious people have never recognised as fear: that of demanding guarantees from God, or from life. It is the prayer that is, at bottom, a negotiation, a plea that nothing change, that no one fall ill, that loss not come, as if faith were an insurance against separation.

It is clinging to a certainty about the beyond, or about divine protection, with the same hand with which one clings to people, so as not to have to feel the uncertainty and the loss that are part of being alive.

But a faith that is a grip is not trust, it is trust's forgery, because true trust demands no guarantees, it gives itself without them, it accepts that life has loss within it and loves it all the same.

The faith that holds without gripping does not ask God to abolish separation, it asks for the courage to pass through it without shattering, and that is a faith of open hands, unlike the other, which prays with a closed fist asking that nothing it loves ever be taken from it, a plea that life, sooner or later, cannot grant.

And there is the grip turned toward the past, which is the most silent of all. It is the inability to let go of what has already gone. The relationship that ended years ago and still occupies the best room in your mind.

The person who died and whose place you keep intact, not out of memory, which is sacred, but out of a refusal to go on living that you confuse with fidelity. There is an enormous difference between honouring the one who is gone and staying clung to the pain of the loss like someone clinging to the last thing that still ties them to you.
The first frees you to love again without betraying anyone. The second keeps the fist closed over an absence, and a hand closed over emptiness can receive nothing more.

Let me linger on this grip of the past, because it is one of those that binds most people without their knowing its name. When we lose someone, whether by death or by the end of a relationship, the pain of the loss becomes, strangely, the last tie that binds us to the person.

As long as it hurts, they are still here, in some way, present in the very absence. And so a part of us clings to the pain, is afraid to let it go, because to let go of the pain seems to let the person depart for good, seems a second loss on top of the first, almost a betrayal.

So one spends years feeding a suffering that no longer serves the love, it serves only the fear of letting go. But the truth, and it is a hard and liberating truth, is that to honour the one who is gone is not to stay bound to the pain of their absence, it is to let their passage through us go on bearing fruit in the life we go on living.
The dead we loved do not ask for our paralysis. They ask, if they ask anything, that what we were with them go on living in us, and a heart paralysed by pain goes on with nothing, it only halts.

There is yet another mask of the grip that almost no one recognises as a grip, because it wears the most touching clothes of all: worry. It is the grip that tightens through anxiety. It does not control the other with orders, it controls them with its own fear.
You stay awake until he arrives, and he knows it, and that loving vigil weighs on him like a chain.

You fall ill with dread when the child takes a risk, and the child learns to hide his risks from you, not because he does not love you, but to spare you the suffering, and so worry, which gave itself as love, drives away instead of drawing near, teaches the other to lie out of kindness.

A person who worries too much seems the most devoted of all, and that is why this mask is so hard to remove: who would tell her that her dread, so clearly love, is also a form of holding? But notice the effect, which is the test as always. The worry that is love strengthens the other, trusts him, accompanies him without weighing on him.

The worry that is a grip weakens him, transmits to him that the world is dangerous and that he is not capable, and binds him to a dull guilt at making her suffer every time he lives. The highest love for the one who departs into the world is not to worry without rest.

It is to trust, which is the hardest and most generous form of loving, because it demands that you let go of the illusion that your dread protects him, when what it does is weigh upon his shoulders his whole life long.

And there is the grip that disguises itself as indispensability, the most cunning of all, because it does not grip the other, it binds them through dependence. Instead of holding the one you love with your hands, you make it so they cannot live without your hands.

You take charge of everything, you solve everything, you become the only one who knows where things are, how they are done, what is needed, and so, without an order, without a visible jealousy, you guarantee that the other cannot leave you because they do not know how to be without you.
You call this caring, and there is much care in it, but underneath dwells the same fear of loss, making its coldest calculation: if he needs me, he will not leave me.

It is an invisible grip, made not of tightening but of manufactured dependence, and its price is double, because it shrinks the other, who never learns to fend for himself, and it imprisons you, who become bound to being indispensable, with no right to tire, no right to fall ill, no right to stop, because you have built a house that collapses if you leave it one day.

Whoever loves toward freedom wants to become, in time, a little dispensable, because they know that the love that stays by choice is worth infinitely more than the love that stays for not knowing how to leave.
The Edge
Now the honesty, without which this lens becomes cruel. Not all nearness is a grip. Not all care is control. Not all pain of losing is a sick fear.

Love truly binds, and that true binding makes loss hurt. That is not the grip, it is the just price of having loved, and whoever never wants to pay that price ends up loving nothing with weight. To want to be near the one you love is healthy. To feel the absence is healthy.

To weep a loss with a pain that seems not to fit in the body is healthy, it is the most healthy thing there is, it is love showing its size after the end. Do not confuse grief with the grip. Grief is the hand opening slowly, hurting because it opens. The grip is the hand that refuses to open and calls its rigidity fidelity.

There is also a care that holds without binding, and one must know how to see it so as not to shame it. The mother who protects her child from a real danger is not the grip, it is the work of being a mother. Whoever cares for someone ill and organises everything around them is not control, it is love in action. The sign, once again, is in the effect.

The care that is love leaves the other larger, more capable, more free in time. The care that is a grip leaves them smaller, more dependent, more diminished from year to year. Look at the one you care for and ask in which direction that person is growing at your side. The answer tells you which of the two hands you have been using.

And there is something that must be said in favour of the very pain of loss, so that this face does not make you ashamed of suffering. To suffer a loss is not weakness, nor lack of faith, nor sick attachment.

It is the price of love, and it is a just price, and whoever wants to avoid it at all costs ends up loving nothing with weight, living a lukewarm life in which nothing hurts because nothing matters. Grief is not the enemy of this chapter. The grip is.

Grief is the hand opening slowly, hurting because it lets go, and that pain is sacred, it is love paying its last homage to the one who departed. Do not confuse your legitimate sadness with the sickness we spoke of. You can weep a loss for years and have no grip at all, if that pain does not paralyse your life nor keep you from going on loving.

And you can shed not a single tear and have the whole grip, if you refuse to let go, if you control so as not to lose, if you suffocate the one you love with the fear of losing them. The measure was never how much it hurts.
It was whether your hand, in the end, opens or closes, whether your love frees or imprisons, whether what you feel serves the other's life or only your own dread.

And I want to add something that saves this edge from turning into hardness. Feeling jealousy, once in a while, does not make you a person caught in the grip. Jealousy is a human emotion, it visits almost everyone, and feeling it is not a defect, it is being part of the species. The question is never feeling, it is what you do with what you feel.

There is an enormous difference between feeling a pang of jealousy and recognising it as an alarm of your own, an insecurity that is yours to tend, and feeling jealousy and turning it into an accusation, an interrogation, a control that makes the other pay for your fear.

The first is emotional honesty, and it can even draw you closer, when you say with humility that you are feeling insecure and that you know it is yours. The second is the grip. The same emotion, two opposite destinies, and what separates them is only this: whether you tend to your fear yourself, or hand it to the other as a bill to be paid.

And it must be said, so that this face does not make you fear connection itself, that needing others is not the grip. We are beings who depend on one another, and that dependence is not weakness, it is the condition of our species, it is what kept us alive and what makes us human.

A person who prides herself on needing no one is not cured of the grip, she is on the other side of the same fear, so afraid of losing that she has decided not to bind herself, so as to have nothing to lose.
That is not freedom, it is a worse prison, that of one who defends herself against love in order to defend herself against loss, and ends up safe from both, alone and dry.

Health is not needing no one, it is needing without gripping, it is depending and letting others depend without that turning into possession, it is the gentle interdependence of one who leans on others and lets them lean on her, without binding anyone. There is an enormous difference between needing the other and clutching them.
To need is human, it is the fabric of shared life. To clutch is fear turning need into a chain.

You can need the one you love greatly and have an open hand, and you can need little and have a closed fist, because the grip is not measured by need, it is measured by the tightening, and there are those who love with great need and no tightening at all, which is, in the end, the most human way to love.
What Disarms This Face

The relational ontology we have already met has, about this face, a particularly liberating word, and it must be said with care so as not to turn it into cheap consolation. Where it is understood that I am because we are, the person you love is not a possession you can lose.

She is a part of the fabric of which you too are made, and that fabric does not come undone because she has a life of her own. On the contrary, it is her own life that keeps alive the thread that binds you. In that way of seeing, no one belongs to anyone, and yet everyone belongs to everyone, because belonging is not clutching, it is being made of the same.

The grip tightens because it believes the other is its own and can flee from it. Where the bond is understood as fabric and not as possession, there is nowhere to flee, because there is not the separation the grip spends its life trying to prevent.

There is here a reversal that can change the very nature of your love, if you let it in. The grip loves out of lack, it loves to fill a hole, it needs the other so as not to feel an emptiness, and so the other becomes a function, a hole-filler, and the love is held hostage to the fear of the hole reappearing.

There is another way to love, born not of lack but of fullness, that loves out of overflow and not out of want, that wants the other not to complete itself but to share a life that was already whole before they arrived.

That way does not grip, because it does not need, and by not gripping it lets the other breathe, and by letting them breathe it is precisely the one that retains most, because people stay where they can breathe and flee from where they suffocate.
It is one of the deepest paradoxes of the life of the heart: whoever grips least is whoever loses least, and whoever grips most is whoever, by gripping, manufactures the very loss they fear.

There is a barer truth still, which these cultures never hid and which we spend our lives hiding from ourselves: everything you love, you will lose. Not as a threat, as a fact. People depart, phases end, children grow and go, bodies die, including yours. The grip lives fleeing from this truth, and it is that flight that wears it out.

On the day you look at it head on, without drama, like one who accepts the law of the house, a strange thing happens. The hand begins to open, not out of despair, but out of wisdom. You love better what you know you will lose, because you stop trying to keep it and begin to be present while it lasts.

The awareness of loss, looked at without terror, is the greatest teacher of presence there is. The grip robs you of the present by trying to hold the future. Open it, and the present is given back to you whole.
I am not asking you to accept this truth all at once, that would be asking too much and too soon. I ask you a smaller thing, one that fits in a gesture.

The next time you feel your hand closing over the one you love, the jealousy rising, the urge to control, the panic of an absence, stop for an instant and ask a simple question: what do I really want for this person, their good, or my own security?

And if the honest answer is your own security, do not condemn yourself, only notice, and then make a small gesture in the opposite direction, let her go to that plan, do not ask the question you were about to ask, bear the absence without filling it with messages.

Each time you open the hand one finger, even afraid, you teach your body that one can love without gripping and that the world does not end, and it is so, finger by finger, and not all at once, that a hand closed for a lifetime learns, slowly, to hold without binding.

There is, about the deepest loss of all, that of the death of those we love, a wisdom in those same cultures that disarms the grip in a way our own way of seeing scarcely allows. Among us, the dead one goes, is cut off, disappears, and grief is the solitary management of that absolute cut. In many African traditions it is not so.

The one who dies does not stop being part of the community, they change place within it. They continue to be named, they continue present, they continue to belong to the living as an ancestor, and as long as there is someone who remembers them and includes them, they are not separated, they are here in another way.

The bond is not broken by death, it is transformed, and love is not left without a recipient, it continues to have somewhere to go. I do not bring you this as a doctrine to adopt, nor do I know what lies beyond death, I have said it already and I say it again.

I bring it to you as another possible way to feel loss, one in which the one who departs does not fall into an absolute void but passes into a different presence, and in which your role is not to cling to the pain to keep them with you, it is to go on weaving them into your life, to include them, to let them be what they can no longer be but continue to be in you.

The grip grips the dead in pain because it believes that only pain retains them. This other way of looking tells it that there are sweeter ways to retain, made of living memory and not of paralysed suffering, and that to let go of the pain is not to let go of the person, it is to let them pass from the place of wound to the place of root.

This is, perhaps, the deepest lesson this face has to give about love entire, and not only about grief. Where the person feels made of bonds, and not an island that possesses a few bonds, loss changes its nature.

It goes on hurting, and it should hurt, but it does not threaten the existence of the one who remains in the same way, because the one who remains has not built their whole identity on the possession of an other. An island that loses its only bridge is isolated and fears sinking.

A person woven of many bonds who loses one of them is wounded, but remains woven, remains held within the fabric, and so can weep the loss without being destroyed by it, can open the hand over the one who departed without feeling left without ground. The grip is the gesture of one who feels themselves an island and treats each bond as the only bridge.

The cure is to discover that you were never an island, that you are made of many connections and not the hostage of a single one, and that love, when it is not the only thing holding you upright, can be loved with open hands, because it no longer carries, alone, the impossible weight of keeping you from falling.
The Final Turn

I will not ask you to love less, that would be asking you to live less. The question with which you close this chapter is another, and more delicate: does your love, today, make the beloved larger or smaller? Does it let her breathe and grow and have a life that does not all pass through you, or does it grip her slowly in the name of not losing her?

And if the answer hurts you, do not use it against yourself. Use it to notice, the next time you feel your hand closing, the fear that lies beneath the gesture. It does not need to be fought. It needs to be seen, called by its right name, and to know that what you are about to call love is, in that instant, the fear of losing.
That alone already changes the temperature of the hand. To see the grip is the beginning of being able to open it.
And do not deceive yourself into thinking that opening the hand is loving less. It is the opposite.

It takes more love to hold someone without gripping them than to bind them, because opening the hand demands that you bear your own anguish without discharging it onto the other, that you trust where you were afraid, that you let live the one you long to keep. The grip is the easy path, the one fear takes on its own.
The open hand is the hard path, the one only love manages when it decides to be greater than the fear that accompanies it.

So, each time you open the hand, you are not loving less, you are loving better, loving in the highest form there is, the one that wants the other's good above its own peace, and it is that, and not the tightening, that is the true measure of how much you love.

There is more for you than this tightening. There is a way of loving that does not live haunted by the end, that holds without binding, that rejoices in the other's life instead of watching over it. That way is not strange to you.

You have felt it already, in rare moments, when the fear lowered its guard and you loved someone with no reckonings and no conditions, only for the joy of their existing. I see that capacity in you. It is beneath the grip, waiting for you to open the hand.

And when you open it, you will discover that the next face of fear does not fear losing a person, but losing the very ground you need in order to live, the bread, the sustenance, what keeps you upright when no one is watching.

pergunta: what are you postponing living, waiting for a security that, when it arrives, you will find is still one step ahead of you?
destaque: It is always winter inside,¦whatever the weather outside.

blocos.texto: Do the sum in your head, right now, without preparing. How much would you have to have in order to feel finally safe? Notice what happened inside you as you answered. Most likely the number rose as you drew closer to it.

Most likely you have already had, at some earlier time of your life, what your version of ten years ago would have called security, and yet you did not feel safe when you got there; you felt only a new line further ahead. That fleeing line is the signature of this face.
I call it the Winter, because it is the fear that the food will not last for the cold that is coming.
What It Protects

Scarcity fears losing its sustenance, the thing that keeps you alive when the world tightens. It is a fear with roots as deep as those of rejection, and of the same age. For almost the whole of human history, winter killed. There was no refrigerator, no guaranteed reserve, no state to cushion the fall.

Whoever had not gathered enough before the cold did not make it through. The body you inherited was built by generations who survived because they were afraid in time, because they stored, because they distrusted the summer's abundance and prepared for the winter's hunger. That fear is an inheritance of survivors.
The trouble is that it goes on working as if every day might be the eve of a famine, even when your pantry is full.

See the winter in a concrete instant, one of those that pass unnoticed because they look like nothing more than good sense. You have received good news, a break, some money you did not expect, a good thing that ought to bring you joy.

And for a second, even before the joy, you feel something else, a reflex to save, to spare, not to spend, to set aside against the bad day that is bound to come. The joy is postponed, almost suspect, as if it were not permitted to you until you are safe, and since you are never safe, the joy is postponed forever. Notice what the winter did there.
It turned a good thing into a worry, a gift into a responsibility, a moment of pleasure into an occasion for precaution.

This is how it robs you of your life, not with great tragedies, but with small reflexes that put out the joy in the very instant it is born, that do not let the good be good without at once turning it into one more reserve against the bad.

A person who does this a thousand times reaches the end of a life full of good things they never truly enjoyed, because each one was received with the reflex to store it instead of the gesture to live it.
That is why scarcity rarely has anything to do with what you actually have. It has to do with a feeling, and the feeling is deaf to what you possess.

There are people with very little who live with the serenity of those who trust there will be enough, and there are people with a great deal who live in a muffled panic, counting, comparing, fearing ruin at every corner. Scarcity is not a condition of the bank account. It is an inner temperature. It is always winter inside, whatever the weather outside.

There is a detail in the origin of this face worth seeing, because it explains why it is so stubborn. The fear of lack was, for almost the whole of history, more useful to survival than trust in abundance. An ancestor who trusted that there would always be food and did not store for the winter died in the first hard winter, and left no descendants.
An ancestor who distrusted, who feared lack even in plenty, who stored as a precaution, that one made it through the winter and had children, to whom the same dread was passed.

You descend, therefore, from those who feared lack, not from those who trusted, and your body inherited a powerful bias toward worry, a finger that always weighs toward the side of fear, because for millennia it was that heavy finger that separated those who lived from those who did not. Your material anxiety is not irrational.
It is the voice of thousands of winters your ancestors survived by having heeded it.

But notice the difference between the voice and the moment in which it speaks, because that is where everything lies. The winter's voice was wise when the winter was real, when there was no guaranteed reserve and not to store was to die.

Today, for many people, that voice goes on speaking with the same urgency in a world that is no longer that one, crying danger where there is no longer any danger of death, demanding accumulation where there is already enough.

It is like a fire alarm that goes on ringing long after the fire has gone out, deafening, tireless, answering a smoke that no longer exists. It does not fall silent by being told to be silent, nor is it convinced by arguments, because it is not rational; it is older than reason.

It falls silent in another way, slowly, by being shown repeatedly, with facts and not with ideas, that the fire has passed, that there is food on the table, that today's winter does not kill as it once killed, that one can spend a little and still stand, that one can give and not be left without.

It is the work of years, not of a day, and it is done with patience, the way you teach a frightened child to trust, not by scolding it for its fear, but by showing it, time after time, that this time it is safe, until the body, which does not believe in words, begins to believe in the repeated experience that, after all, there is enough.
The Turn

What you call prudence, foresight, feet planted firmly on the ground, is often the winter governing you. And it is a treacherous face, because society as a whole applauds its symptoms. We praise those who save, those who work without pause, those who never allow themselves a luxury, those who always put safety ahead of desire.
These virtues exist, and we will return to them. But beneath them there often dwells a fear that never rests and that confuses being alive with being safe.

Look at what the winter makes you do. It makes you postpone life to a future in which you will finally be safe, a future that never arrives because the line of safety recedes whenever you draw near. It makes you work beyond what is needed, not for the pleasure of the work, but for the momentary relief the effort gives the alarm.
It makes you look at what others have, not with desire for what they have, but with a tightening that what exists in the world is limited and that what is theirs was taken from you.

Scarcity turns life into the permanent management of reserves against a catastrophe that rarely comes, and in doing so it robs you of the one truly scarce thing you have, which is the time in which you are alive.
Here is the turn that changes everything. You call this way of looking at the world realistic, and it prides itself on being realistic, on not deceiving itself, on being prepared for the worst.

But notice something: a person caught in the winter does not see the world as it is; they see it as scarcer than it is, more dangerous than it is, more miserly than it is. Fear does not make them realistic, it makes them pessimistic, and pessimism is not lucidity, it is a filter that darkens everything.

They call prudence what is a distortion, and since the distortion keeps them worried, and the worry looks responsible, and responsibility is praised, they never suspect that they are seeing wrongly. They see little, and call it seeing clearly, and spend their life preparing for a famine that, in most cases, never comes with the face they imagined.
How It Operates Within

The mechanism of this face lives off a sum that never closes. However much you gather, the number that would give you rest is always a little above what you have, because it is not the number that gives the rest, it is an inner sense of sufficiency that the number never produces.

People caught in the winter believe that peace will come with the next amount, and so they chase it, and when they get there the peace is not there, only a new amount further ahead. It is a race after a horizon, and the horizon, by definition, moves away at the same pace you run.

There is a particular mechanism inside this face that must be named: comparison. The winter does not measure what you have in absolute terms. It measures it against what others have, because its deep model is that of a limited resource that is shared out. If there is a fixed amount of bread, the bread that is another's is bread you went without.

This logic made sense in a village with a single harvest. It makes far less sense with most of the things we desire today, which are not used up when another has them: knowledge, affection, creation, meaning. But the winter does not distinguish.

It applies the logic of bread to everything, and turns life into a muffled competition in which the happiness of others takes something from you, even when it takes nothing. That is why a person caught in this face cannot rejoice fully in the good fortune of someone they love. A part of them reads another's fortune as a subtraction from their own.

Notice how this comparison poisons even the goods that are not shared out. You see someone flourish, create something beautiful, be happy in a way that takes absolutely nothing from you, and even so you feel a tightening, a small diminishment, as though their light cast a shadow over you.

This is not malice, it is the winter applying its pantry arithmetic to things that are not a pantry. Happiness is not a cake with a counted number of slices, in which another's slice is a slice you went without. It is more like a flame, which is not put out by lighting another, which even multiplies when it is shared.

But the winter does not know how to see flames, it only knows how to count loaves, and so it lives impoverished in the midst of abundance, unable to warm itself at the fire of others because it reads it as a theft of its own.

The winter has, moreover, a cost that is almost never reckoned: the inability to receive. Whoever lives in scarcity mode is so busy defending what they have, saving, taking precautions, that they become incapable of letting abundance in even when it knocks at the door. They refuse gifts, distrust generosity, find the hidden threat in every good thing.

It is a heart closed so as not to let out the little it believes it has, and in closing itself so as not to lose, it also closes itself so as not to receive. The winter turns the soul into a locked pantry, and a locked pantry starves to death beside an orchard.
And there is a last piece, the most costly of all: the winter robs the pleasure of what one already has.

A person caught in this face may be sitting in the midst of everything they worked a lifetime for, and be unable to enjoy any of it, because their attention is not on what they have, it is on what they might lose, on what might run short, on what others have more of.

It is like eating an entire meal while thinking of tomorrow's hunger, and so going hungry today in the midst of plenty. The winter does not only impoverish the future it fears. It impoverishes the present it possesses, by refusing it the attention that would turn it into enjoyment.
And this is perhaps its most absurd theft: to make someone go hungry who has food on the table, only because their mind is in the pantry instead of on the plate.
There is a mechanism in the winter worth seeing, because it explains why it worsens, rather than heals, with time.

Each time you gather more, what you have grown used to rises too, and the new level quickly becomes the normal, and the normal no longer soothes, because only what is above the normal soothes, and the above-the-normal always shifts whenever you reach it.
So the winter adapts to everything you earn, swallows each conquest and asks for the next, and the person lives in a race in which the finish line advances at the same pace they do.

That is why what ten years ago would have seemed to you the security of your dreams, today, now that you have it, does not soothe you, because your eye no longer sees it as much, it sees it as the minimum, and the hunger has moved further ahead.

Whoever does not know this mechanism spends their life thinking that peace will come with the next step, and climbs, and climbs, and the peace does not come, because the peace was never in the step, it was in an inner decision to look at what one has as sufficient, a decision that no climb makes for you and that many only learn to make once they have climbed everything there was and discovered, at the top, the same hunger they began with at the bottom.
There is also a personal root of the winter worth searching for in your own history, because not all inner scarcity is only the inheritance of the species, much of it is the inheritance of a home.

A child who grew up hearing that there was not enough money, who saw their parents in distress over the bills, who learned early that to ask was to be a burden, that child engraved in the body an equation that follows them long after the bills already come out even: that there is always lack, that the world is tight, that to relax is dangerous.

They may have today everything they need and go on living with the tightness of one who has nothing, because the winter they carry is not that of their present life, it is that of the kitchen of their childhood, and that one is not paid off with any money, because it is not a debt from outside, it is a memory from within.

To recognize where your cold comes from, whether it comes from a real lack of today or from an old lack that has already passed but stayed engraved, is the beginning of being able to warm it, because it changes the remedy: today's lack is cured by changing the conditions, the old lack is cured by seeing that it has already passed and that the body goes on trembling from a cold that no longer blows.
The Gallery of Masks

In money, scarcity dresses in an obvious way, but this is the least interesting of its masks, and therefore the easiest to dispatch. It is anxiety over bills that does not match the reality of the bills. It is the inability to spend on pleasure without guilt, even when there is ample slack.

It is the person who has enough to live well for an entire lifetime and who goes on living as if ruin awaited them around the corner. Money is only the screen on which this face projects its film. The film is always the same, and it is called it will not be enough.

But notice something about this mask of money, because it reveals the nature of the whole face. Two people with exactly the same bank account can live one in panic and the other in peace, and the difference is not in the account, it is in each one's relationship with lack.

This proves, better than any argument, that scarcity is not a quantity, it is a relationship. It is not cured by having more, it is cured by changing the relationship with what one has and with what might run short.

That is why earning more almost never soothes the winter: the person rises a level, and the alarm rises with them, and the new level brings its own new fears, and the peace they thought they were buying with money does not come, because it was not for sale, it was something built within, in the place where it is decided whether the world is miserly or generous.
In the body and in food, the winter has an old and stubborn mask, above all in those who lived, or whose parents lived, times of real lack.

It is the inability to throw food away, the plate filled too full and eaten to the end even when the hunger is gone, the pantry stocked as a precaution, a relationship with eating that is not about today's body but about yesterday's hunger, one's own or inherited.

The body that went hungry, or that carries the memory of generations who did, does not trust abundance, it eats like one who does not know when it will eat again, it stores like one who awaits the winter.

There is no gluttony here, there is fear, the winter applied to the most basic of sustenances, and to recognize it thus, as fear and not as lack of will, is the beginning of being able to treat it with compassion instead of with the severity with which the relationship with food is usually treated, which is almost never about food and almost always about a safety that was missing.
In affection, the winter is the saddest mask, because it applies the logic of the pantry to love, which is the thing that least withstands it.

It is those who love while counting, who give expecting the exact return, who take offense at every gesture not repaid, who treat tenderness as a scarce good to be shared out and not as a thing that grows by being given.
It is the person who cannot love in abundance because they are afraid of spending love, of giving too much of it and being left without, of investing in someone who might not give back.
And so they love little, measure what they give, hold themselves back, and end up, in the end, poor in love in the midst of people who would have loved them had they risked loving first.

Love, unlike money, multiplies by being given, but the winter does not believe this, it counts it as it counts coins, and by counting it thus, it dies of want with the vault full, without ever having discovered that the only way to have much love is to give it without counting.
There is a small form of this mask that poisons the relationships of everyday life and that is almost never seen as scarcity: the stinginess of praise.

It is those who find it hard to acknowledge the merit of others, to give a word of praise, to admit that someone did well, as if the praise they give were something they lose, as if acknowledging another's worth diminished their own. Notice the strangeness of this.

A word of praise costs nothing, takes nothing from you, and yet the winter holds it back, measures it, gives it drop by drop, because in its model of the world everything is scarce, even merit, and the merit it acknowledges in another seems to it merit withdrawn from itself.

So a person caught in this face becomes miserly with recognition, unable to rejoice out loud in those around them, and creates about themselves a cold climate where no one feels seen, all because of a stinginess that does not even save money, it saves words that would have cost nothing to give.

Learning to praise generously, to acknowledge the good in others without fear, to give praise like one who has plenty, is one of the simplest and most revealing trainings against the winter, because it shows, in a gesture at no cost, that the scarcity you feel is not in the things of the world, it is in a hand that has learned to close itself even over that which giving does not empty.
And there is the winter in envy, which is its most naked portrait, because it disguises itself as no prudence at all.

Envy is scarcity applied to another's fortune: if the world has a fixed amount of good, the good that fell to another was good that I went without, and so the happiness of others, instead of gladdening me, tightens me, as though what was given to them had been taken from me.

It is one of the loneliest emotions there is, because it drives the person away precisely from those with whom they might have rejoiced, and it is one of the most stubborn, because it hides itself in shame and so is never looked at head on.

But when you look at it, you see that it is not malice, it is the winter, it is the old fear that there is not enough projecting itself onto the fortune of others, reading it as a subtraction from your share.

And you see too its cure, which is the same as that of this whole face: to discover that the good is not a cake of counted slices, that another's happiness takes no happiness from you, that there is sun enough for all, and that you can rejoice in the light that falls on those you love without feeling in it a shadow over yourself, because it was never a shadow, it was only the winter doing, once again, its mistaken accounting of a world it sees as far poorer than it is.

And there is even a spiritual winter, a scarcity applied to the sacred itself, that grips many religious people without their knowing its name. It is the sense that there is not enough grace, that forgiveness is scarce and must be earned through suffering, that God gives drop by drop and each crumb must be paid for dearly.

It is living faith the way one lives poverty, afraid that it will not be enough, accumulating merits against a tight judgment, treating the divine as if it were as miserly as the inner winter of the one who imagines it.
Now, almost all the great spiritual traditions say exactly the opposite, they speak of an abundance without measure, of a grace that does not run out, of a love that is not counted.

Whoever lives faith in the mode of the winter did not hear that part, they projected onto heaven the scarcity they carry on earth, and turned what ought to be the greatest of openings into the tightest of accountings.

Here too the cure is the same: to see that the scarcity was not in things, nor even in the sacred, it was in an inner temperature that froze everything in its passing, including that which, by its nature, was without end.
In work, the winter is the inability to stop. Not the dedication of one who loves what they do, which is another thing, but the engine that does not switch off because stopping wakes the alarm.
It is the woman who has already proven what she had to prove and goes on running, who takes a holiday with the laptop in her bag, who feels rest as a threat instead of a right.

Beneath her admirable productivity there often dwells a child who learned that she was only safe while she was useful, and who goes on buying her safety with work, without ever reaching the day when she has paid enough. And there is a price in the body, because the body was not made to live in permanent alarm.

A system tuned for the winter lives in constant tension, and constant tension wears down, sickens, exhausts, and at a certain point the body presents the bill the will refused to see, forcing to stop the one who did not know how to stop by choice.

In relationships, and this is the most surprising mask, scarcity appears as a fear that affection is limited. It is those who cannot rejoice when the one they love gives attention to another person, because in their deep model love is a finite resource that is shared out, and what went to another was withheld from them.

It is the mother who feels jealous of the tenderness her child gives the grandmother. It is the friend who cannot bear that her friend has another friend. It is the reading of love as a cake cut into slices and not as a thing that grows by being given.

Scarcity applied to the heart is one of the saddest forms of this face, because it impoverishes precisely the one good that multiplies when it is divided. A mother who loves two children does not love each one half. She loves each one whole, because love is not shared out, it is generated.

But the winter does not believe this, it counts affection as it counts bread, and so it lives afraid that the love another gives to someone else is love taken from it, when it is, in truth, proof that that heart has plenty to spare.
And there is a form of the affective winter that poisons relationships without ever being seen, because it disguises itself as generosity: always giving on account.

It is the person who gives much, who cares, who devotes themselves, but who gives like one making an investment, awaiting the return, and who takes offense, with a muffled bitterness, when the return does not come in the measure they expected.

They keep the account of what they did, of what they gave, of what others owe them, and present it, sooner or later, in the form of complaint or resentment, recalling all they did that no one acknowledged.
This is the winter applied to giving, turning the gift into a loan at interest, because in its model of scarcity nothing is given for free, everything must come back, or one is left the loser.

And whoever receives feels the weight of the hidden account, feels that what was given to them was not given, it was lent, and the gratitude that would have been born of a free gift dies under the weight of a debt they never asked for.
True generosity gives and lets go, keeps no account, charges nothing, because it springs from a sense of abundance, that there is plenty, that giving does not empty.

The winter cannot give like that, it always gives measuring, recording, awaiting the return, and so it impoverishes even its own generosity, turning what could have been a clean gift into a transaction in which it ends up, in the end, always with the sense of having given more than it received.

And there is the deepest mask of all, the scarcity of time and of life, which is hardly seen because it dresses itself as urgency. It is the permanent sense that there are not enough hours, that you are always behind, that life is a list of tasks against a clock that runs. This hurry is the winter applied to existence itself.
It makes you consume your time saving time, run through your day preparing a better day, cross life making reserves against a scarcity of life that the hurry itself is manufacturing.

It is the one winter that fulfills itself: whoever is afraid that life will not be enough lives in such a way that, in the end, life truly was not enough, not for lack of time, but for never having been present in the time they had.

Notice the fine perversity of this last mask, because it catches many who believe themselves safe from the others. A person may be not at all miserly with money, may even be generous, and be miserly with time in the same way, always running, unable to linger, feeling each moment of leisure as a waste of a resource that flees.

They treat the hours as the miser treats the coins, counting them, fearing to lose them, unable to spend them on anything that does not produce. And the result is a life crossed at a run, always on the way to the next moment, never inside the present moment, a life in which one was physically present in everything and truly present in almost nothing.
In the end, that person will not have lost life for lack of time. They will have lost it for fear of losing it, which is the most absurd of all the ways of losing it.

There is yet another mask of the winter that dresses itself as virtue and is therefore rarely questioned: the inability to enjoy. It is the person who worked a lifetime and does not let themselves rest, who has the means to give themselves a pleasure and always postpones it to a later that does not come, who feels free joy as a waste and repose as a guilt.

They call it being hardworking, being serious, not being a spendthrift, and there is dignity in those things, but beneath them there often dwells the winter, which will not let one spend even what is left over, because spending, for it, is to lower the guard, and to lower the guard is danger.

So a person may reach the end of life having gathered much and lived little, having saved everything and enjoyed almost nothing, and this is one of the saddest forms of this face, because it does not catch them in poverty, it catches them in plenty, and shows that the winter is not a question of how much one has, it is an inner prohibition against receiving one's own life, a stinginess that is not only with money, it is with pleasure, with rest, with joy, with everything the alarm reads as a luxury you cannot allow yourself.

And there is the mask of the winter in generosity refused, which is its most paradoxical portrait. The winter does not store only for itself, often it is even generous with others and miserly only with itself, unable to receive what others wish to give it.

It refuses the gift, the invitation, the help, the care, with a no that disguises itself as not wanting to be a bother but that is, at bottom, the same face closing the door, now to what comes in instead of to what goes out.
Because to receive is to be in debt, is to depend, is to admit a lack, and the winter would rather go in need than admit that it needs.

So the person caught in this face stays alone in their cold in the midst of people who would have reached out a hand to them, not out of pride, though it looks like pride, but out of an old fear that accepting today's help will leave them without defense tomorrow.

Learning to receive, to let oneself be cared for, to say yes to an outstretched hand, is for whoever lives in the winter as difficult and as healing as learning to give is for whoever lives in the grip, because it breaks the loneliness of the locked pantry and shows the body, with a fact, that one can depend on others and be more safe, not less.
The Edge
Here honesty is even more necessary, because scarcity, unlike other faces, often responds to real dangers, and it would be a cruelty to treat all prudence as sickness.

There are people for whom scarcity is not an inherited fear, it is the reality of Tuesday. There are bills that truly do not come out even at the end of the month, winters that really do kill, precarities in which material anxiety is not an inner film, it is a correct reading of a hard life.

To these, this chapter does not come to say that the fear is imagined. It comes to say the opposite: that the weight they carry is just, that their vigilance is a survival competence, and that the cure is not to look inward, it is to change the conditions, when the conditions can change.
To confuse real scarcity with a problem of mindset is one of the favorite cruelties of those who have never gone hungry. Let us not fall into it.

I want to insist on this, because today much lies wrapped in spirituality are sold. Those who have little are told that their lack is a matter of attitude, that it is enough to think of abundance to attract it, that poverty is a problem of mindset.

This is a cruelty, and it is a lie, and it serves above all to ease the conscience of those who have much, telling those who have little that the fault is theirs. Real scarcity exists, it is unjust, and it is not solved from within, it is solved by changing the conditions, with work, with solidarity, with justice.

This chapter does not speak of that scarcity. It speaks of the inner winter that persists even when the outer conditions have already changed, of the fear of lack that continues after the lack has passed.
To distinguish the two is not a detail, it is a matter of decency, and whoever confuses them either understood nothing, or has an interest in not understanding.

And even far from poverty, there is a prudence that is wisdom and not fear. Saving for a child to study is love with sound accounts. Not spending what one does not have is dignity. Preparing for winter when the winter is real is intelligence, it is what our ancestors did well and why we are here. The difference, again, is in the inner effect.

Wise prudence leaves you at peace after the decision is taken. The winter never leaves you at peace, because its function is not to solve the problem, it is to keep the alarm switched on. If you save and then rest, it is prudence. If you save and the rest never comes, whatever the amount, it is the winter governing you beneath a respectable name.

And there is a sobriety that is no fear at all, and it must be honored so as not to confuse everything with sickness. There are those who live with little by choice, out of values, out of not wanting to tie life to the possession of things, and that simplicity is the opposite of the winter, it is freedom from it.

The winter clings to what it has, afraid to lose it; chosen sobriety lets go of the superfluous with lightness, because it has discovered that it needs less than it thought in order to be happy. One lives in tightness in the midst of plenty; the other lives in peace with little.
Do not confuse those who have little out of fear with those who have little out of wisdom, because they are exact opposites, though from the outside they look alike.

The sign, once again, is inner: the sobriety that is freedom leaves you at peace, generous, able to enjoy what you have; the winter that is fear leaves you in tension, clutching, unable to take pleasure.

There is a poverty that is riches, that of one who has freed themselves from the hunger to have, and there is a riches that is poverty, that of one who has much and lives in panic of losing it. What decides which is which was never the quantity of things, it was the temperature of the heart that has them.
What Disarms This Face

Relational ontology offers this face one of its deepest corrections, and it is not a sentimental correction, it is almost an economic one. Where one lives the I am because we are, a person's security is not stored in their private pantry, it is distributed across the network of those around them. You do not gather alone against the winter.

The winter is faced by all, and so no one needs to accumulate as if they were alone against the cold. Sharing, in that way of living, is not moral generosity, it is the very architecture of survival: I give today to those who are hungry because I know that, on my day of hunger, I will eat of what another gave.
The reserve is not in the granary of one, it is in the bond between many, and a bond is not used up when it is used, it is strengthened.

There is here an inversion that can change your inner temperature, if you let it in. The winter tells you that security is won by closing the hand and gathering. This other wisdom tells you that the deepest security is built by opening the hand and connecting.

Not because opening the hand is more virtuous, but because a life bound to others withstands winters that a solitary life, however full its pantry, does not withstand. Those who have much and no one are more exposed to the cold than those who have little and many. The scarcity that truly kills is not that of goods. It is that of bonds.
And that one, unlike the other, is cured by giving, not by storing.

It is worth looking at how this worked, and still works, in the communities where this wisdom was kept. In a village where one person's harvest is shared with all, no one goes hungry when their own harvest fails, because they will eat of the others', as the others ate of theirs in the good years.

The reserve against the winter is not locked in the granary of each one, at the mercy of a roof that collapses or of a theft, it is spread across the whole community, in the custom of giving and receiving, which is infinitely more resistant than any individual pantry. An individual pantry, however full, can burn, can be stolen, can fall short.

A network of giving does not all burn at once. The winter, in its solitude, accumulates against catastrophe and is left, even so, exposed, because it is alone. Relational wisdom distributes, and by distributing it is more secure, because true security was never having much, it was not being alone.

I do not ask you to stop taking precautions. I ask you to notice where you have put your reserve. If you have put it all in the granary and none in the bond, then however full the granary may be you will go on being cold, because a granary keeps you no company in the winter, and a bond does.

And I ask of you a small experiment, one that goes against the winter at its most stubborn point: the next time you feel the tightening of lack, make a gesture of giving, however small. Give something, offer, share, precisely in the moment when the alarm orders you to close your hand. Notice what happens inside.

Almost always, the gesture of giving loosens the tightening faster than the gesture of storing, because it gives your body the proof, against the winter's thesis, that you have plenty, that you can give and still stand, and that proof, repeated, teaches the alarm what no amount ever taught it: that there is enough.

There is an even simpler exercise, and it is the exact opposite of what the winter does all day long. The winter always looks at what is missing, that is its natural attention, the accounting of what there is not. Try, for a time, to train the contrary attention, that of what there is.

Not as a wellness technique, but as the correction of an error of vision, because the winter really does see wrongly, it sees the world poorer than it is, and attention to what there is is not naivety, it is adjusting the lens so you can see what was there and you did not see.
Notice what you have, what you received today, what is already yours and the alarm does not count because it is busy counting what is missing.

Not to convince yourself that all is well when it is not, that would be a lie, but to return to your inner account the entries the winter erased, because an account that records only what is missing always gives a negative balance, however much one has, and an account that also records what there is begins, often, to close in numbers so much in its favor that they frighten.

This is not deceiving yourself, it is counting straight, and counting straight, for whoever lives in the winter, is already a small revolution, because a whole life was counted crookedly, always adding up the lacks and forgetting the surpluses.

And there is a last thing to say about what warms this face, and it ties it to the heart of the whole book. The winter is, at bottom, a form of loneliness. It is the separate self, the island, that feels alone against the scarcity of the world and so must accumulate its own reserve against its own winter, counting on no one.

Where a person feels made of bonds, and not an island defending its pantry, the very notion of scarcity changes, because what sustains you ceases to be what you stored alone and becomes the network you belong to, and a network is infinitely richer than any individual pantry, because in it the plenty of some covers the lack of others, in a back-and-forth that never runs out.
The deepest scarcity, the one not even money cures, is that of one who feels alone before life.

And the deepest abundance, the one not even poverty can wholly take away, is that of one who feels a part, connected, belonging, because that person knows, in the body and not in theory, that they do not face the winter alone, and it is that certainty, and not the size of the reserve, that finally warms the old cold this chapter spoke of.
The Final Turn

The question with which you close this chapter is not how much you need to have, because that question belongs to the winter itself and never has an answer. It is another: what are you postponing living while you wait for a security that, when it comes, you will discover is still one step ahead?

Let the question stay. Do not turn it into an order to spend what you do not have nor to let go of all prudence overnight. Use it with more patience. The next time you feel the tightening of lack, when the inner account will not close however much you gather, do just one thing: notice the temperature.

See that it is winter inside even with the pantry full, and that the cold does not come from what you lack, it comes from an old alarm that confuses being alive with being safe. To see the winter is the first step toward being able to warm it.

And there is a question that, if you ask it honestly, measures the size of your winter better than any bank account: when was the last time you felt you had enough? Not that you had much, which is rare for everyone, but simply enough, sufficient, a sense that, for now, all was well, that you could stop gathering and simply live.

For many people caught in this face, the answer is never, or so long ago they no longer remember, because the winter never declares enough, it always postpones it to an amount further ahead that, when it arrives, is no longer enough.

Learning to say enough, to recognize a point at which you already have enough and can rest, is not laziness nor imprudence, it is the greatest possible victory over the winter, because it takes from it what it lives on, the endless flight forward, and returns you to the only place where sufficiency ever existed, which was not a number in the future, it was a decision in the present.

There is more for you than this endless counting. There is a way of living that trusts there will be enough, not out of unawareness, but out of having made peace with the one true certainty, which is that nothing is guaranteed and even so life gives itself, day after day, and almost always arrives.
You have already tasted moments like that, when the fear lowered and you received an entire day without spending it defending yourself against it. I see that capacity in you, beneath the frost.

And when it grows, you will discover that the next face fears not that the bread will run short, but that the ground will, the predictable, the map, everything you cling to so as not to have to look head on at the fact that you never truly know what comes next.

blocos.texto: Think of the last time someone said to you "let's see what happens" and notice what you felt. For many people, those words are a relief. For you, they may have been a small jolt, an immediate urge to make a plan, to close the matter, to know now. If you know that jolt, you know this face. It fears losing no one and nothing in particular.

It fears something more abstract and therefore harder to name: it fears the unknown. It fears not knowing. It fears the ground of the future, which is always made of mist, and it spends its life trying to build upon the mist something solid where it can set its feet. I call it the Fortress.
What It Protects

Uncertainty fears the loss of the predictable, of the map, of the firm ground to walk on without jolts. It is a fear that seems very civilised, very grown-up, and yet it comes from the same ancient place as the others. An animal that does not know what comes next is an animal in danger.

Knowing where the water is, where the predator comes from, when the rain arrives, that is the difference between living and dying. The body learned that predictability is safety and that surprise is threat, and so the unknown, even the harmless unknown, sets off in it a muffled alarm. Your need to know is not a quirk.
It is an ancient system doing the work it has always done, which was to keep you alive in a world where not knowing could cost you your life.
Catch the Fortress on any evening, at its most common work. Everything is fine. The day went well, the people you love are home, there is no threat in sight. And yet you do not rest.

Your mind has already gone off to tomorrow, to the week, to what could go wrong, and it runs through, without your asking, a list of things left unresolved, of scenarios to guard against, of possible dangers.

The body is on the sofa, the mind is in an emergency meeting about a future that has not yet arrived and that perhaps will never arrive as you imagine it. You lose the evening, which was good, preparing a day that is not yet, and when that day comes, you will lose it too, preparing the next one. Notice the gentle absurdity of this.
There is no fire, and the Fortress keeps you on guard beside the bucket, all night long, against a blaze that is not burning.

This is the most constant price of this face, not the great crises, but the thousand calm evenings it will not let you inhabit, because it is always watching what is coming, and what is coming, when it comes, is almost always less than what it feared, and the peace of the evenings, that peace, does not come back.

What changed was the world, not the system. Today most of the uncertainties you face do not kill you. Not knowing how a conversation will go, a project, a year, a decision, none of this is the predator in the thicket. But the body cannot tell it apart from the predator, and it treats the mist of the future with the same urgency it would treat a real threat.

The Fortress is what you build so as not to have to feel that urgency. Walls of plans, of routines, of control, of rules, all to create the illusion that you know what is coming, and so to keep the alarm quiet one more day.

Notice a difference between this face and the others, because it explains why the Fortress is so hard to let go of. The other faces fear the loss of something concrete, the group, the person, the bread. This one fears something that cannot be eliminated at all, because uncertainty is not an accident of life, it is the very condition of being alive in time.

No one knows what comes next, no one, however much they plan, and that is a law of existence, not a flaw to be corrected. The Fortress has declared war on something that cannot be defeated, and so its war has no end.

It can win battle after battle, cover scenario after scenario, and uncertainty is still there, intact, because it was never in an enemy that can be beaten, it was in the nature of the future, which is made of mist by definition and not by carelessness.

It is worth distinguishing two things the Fortress confuses, because it is in that confusion that it loses itself: control and influence. There are things over which you have influence, and there are things over which you have control, and they are very different.

You can influence your health with what you eat and how you live, but you do not control it, because a body does what it does. You can influence the outcome of a piece of work with your effort, but you do not control it, because a thousand things depend on it that are not in your hands.

You can influence how a child grows, but you do not control it, because he is a free person. Almost everything that matters in life is in the realm of influence, not of control, and health lies in doing your part well, the part of influence, and handing over the rest, the part that was never yours.

The Fortress does not accept this division, it wants to control what it can only influence, and so it lives in a perpetual frustration, trying to hold in its hands a thing that, by definition, slips through its fingers. Peace is not in controlling more.

It is in knowing, with clarity, what is yours to do and what is not, in putting all your strength into your part and in letting go, with a kind of trust, of everything else, which was never at your disposal however much you fret.

And there is an irony at the heart of this face that matters to see, because it disarms its own promise. The Fortress promises you that, if you control enough, you will be safe, and yet the people who control the most are among the most anxious there are, not the most tranquil.

If control gave the safety it promises, whoever controlled most would be whoever lived most at peace, and it is exactly the opposite: the more you control, the more anxious you become, because the more things come to depend on you, the more fronts you have to watch, the more you notice everything that could still go wrong.
Control does not cure anxiety, it feeds it, because each thing you bring under your control becomes one more thing you are responsible for, one more thing that could fail you.

The safety the Fortress seeks in control lies, after all, in the opposite place, in the capacity to let go, to trust, to let a part of life run without your supervision, and that is the thing the Fortress least knows how to do, and so it spends its life building walls in search of a peace that would only come from letting them fall.
The Turn

What you call being organised, responsible, prudent, having your feet firmly on the ground, is often the Fortress governing you. And it is one of the most praised faces, because uncertainty terrifies everyone and whoever controls it seems strong.

We admire the one who has everything planned, who is never caught off guard, who has an answer for every eventuality. We do not see the price that person pays within, the tiredness of never being able to lower the guard, the rigidity that prevents her from welcoming the unexpected when the unexpected brings, as it so often does, the best of life.

Look at what the Fortress makes you refuse. It refuses you spontaneity, because spontaneity is uncertainty coming in through a door you did not control. It refuses you change, even good change, because all change is a passage through the unknown.

It refuses you the rest of not knowing, which is a deep rest and one known only to those who have made peace with the mist. The person who lives in the Fortress is always working, even when she seems still, because her mind is permanently anticipating scenarios, preparing responses, plugging holes through which the unforeseen might enter.
It is an invisible and infinite work, because the future produces uncertainty faster than any plan can eliminate it.

And here is the turn that hurts. The Fortress promises you control, and what it gives you, in truth, is a smaller life. Because the only way to reduce uncertainty is to reduce life, and the Fortress reduces life without telling you. You do not take that trip, because it is unpredictable. You do not accept that opportunity, because you did not plan it.
You do not meet that person, you do not change that thing, you do not risk the new, because the new is always uncertain.

So, battle by battle, the Fortress goes on walling in your life, and every wall that protects you from a surprise is also a wall that closes off a possibility, and after years you live in a fortress so safe and so small that it is almost no longer a life, it is a refuge, and the refuge, which should have guarded life, ends up replacing it.

And notice what the Fortress does to the good surprise, because it is here that you see best what it costs you. Life brings, now and then, gifts you did not ask for or plan, a chance meeting, an unexpected invitation, a turn of events that opens a door you did not even know existed.
Almost everything best that has happened to you, if you think about it, arrived that way, without your having planned it, through a crack that opened in the plan.

But the Fortress does not know how to receive a surprise, even a good one, because a surprise is uncertainty, it is something that came in without passing through control, and so its first reaction to the unexpected, even the good unexpected, is alarm, mistrust, the urge to refuse it or to fit it quickly into some plan that tames it.

So, without knowing it, the Fortress closes the very door through which the greatest joys of life would enter, joys that almost never come through the expected door, and condemns itself to an existence without surprises, which is the same as saying without almost anything that makes a life worth living, because the predictable, however safe, is rarely what fills us, and what fills us, almost always, is what we did not see coming.
How It Operates Within

The mechanism of this face lives on a promise that is never kept: the promise that, if you plan enough, you will finally arrive at certainty. The Fortress believes that uncertainty is a problem with a solution, and that the solution is more information, more preparation, more control.

So it plans, and the plan soothes for a moment, and then the future presents a variable the plan did not foresee, and the Fortress's response is not to accept that the future is like this, it is to make a bigger plan.
It grows wall upon wall, and the mist is never left out, because the mist is not out there, it is in the very nature of the time that has not yet come.

There is a cruel mechanism inside this face, and it is called rumination. The mind caught in uncertainty does not rest examining a problem, it circles it without advancing, it produces the same thought over and over with the illusion that, by giving it one more turn, it will reach a certainty that closes it.

It never reaches it, because the question has no right answer, it has only possible futures, and no amount of thought turns a possible future into a known future. Rumination is the Fortress building walls inside its own head, corridors and corridors of worry that protect against nothing and that consume the energy real life was asking for something else.

It is worth seeing the trick that rumination plays on you, because it is subtle. While you think and rethink a problem, you feel you are doing something about it, working to solve it, and so rumination disguises itself as responsibility, as taking care of the matter.
But there is a difference between thinking about a problem and ruminating on it, and it is easy to see: thought advances, arrives at a decision, at an action, at a step, and then it stops.

Rumination does not advance, it circles, returns to the same point, repeats itself, and after an hour you are exactly where you began, only more tired and more frightened. Thought is a tool, you use it and put it down.

Rumination is the tool running empty, wearing you out without cutting anything, giving you the sensation that you are working when you are only being consumed. Recognising the difference is halfway to being able to switch off the machine that turns without grinding.

Rigidity is the other cost, and it is more visible from outside than from within. Whoever lives in the Fortress needs things to be a certain way, and the deviation from that way, however small, provokes a disproportionate unease.

The plan that changes at the last minute, the routine that is broken, the rule someone does not respect, all of this is not a minor inconvenience, it is a crack in the wall, and each crack lets in a thread of the mist the Fortress exists to keep out. That is why these people seem, at times, difficult, controlling, incapable of flowing. They are not.
They are terrified of something no one around them sees, because the thing is not in the room, it is in the future, and they live more in the future than in the present they have in front of them.

There is one last piece, and it is the saddest, because it concerns the lost present. The Fortress lives so busy protecting the future that it almost never inhabits the now. You are at a dinner with those you love, and a part of you is organising the week. You are on holiday, and your mind is already preparing the return.

You are living a good moment, and instead of living it, you worry about how to keep it, how to ensure it continues, what to do if it ends. So the present, which was the only thing you truly had, drains away through your fingers, spent managing a future that, when it arrives, will also be lived managing the next future.

It is a whole life spent in the antechamber of life, waiting for uncertainty to end so that it can finally be lived, without seeing that uncertainty never ends, and that the antechamber where it waited was, itself, life passing by.

There is a phrase that is the secret engine of this face, and perhaps you recognise it because you say it often within: what if. What if it goes wrong, what if he gets sick, what if I lose my job, what if I say the wrong thing, what if I am not able.

The what if is the Fortress's factory of futures, and it works without rest, producing scenarios of disaster with a realism that makes them seem probable when they are only possible. Notice what the what if does to your body.

It does not distinguish an imagined danger from a real one, and so, when you imagine disaster vividly, your body reacts as if it were happening, and you suffer the pain of a thing that did not happen and that, in the overwhelming majority of cases, will never happen.
So whoever lives in the what if suffers a thousand disasters that never come, and pays in advance a price for catastrophes that life, almost always, does not charge.

It is said that whoever fears suffers twice, and it is literally true: once in the imagination, always, and again in reality, rarely, and the second almost never arrives, but the first never fails. The Fortress believes that anticipating disaster prepares it for disaster.
What it does, in most cases, is make it live the disaster in advance, with none of the advantages of its being real, with all the pain and none of the facts.

There is a second piece in the mechanism, and it is the one that makes the Fortress so exhausting without its seeing why: control is insatiable exactly as comparison and recognition were, in the other faces.

Each thing you manage to control soothes for an instant, and in the next instant your attention leaps to the next thing to control, and there is never a point of arrival, because the number of things that can go wrong is infinite and your control is finite.

It is a war on many fronts at once, in which closing one front opens attention to another, and peace never comes because peace would require all the fronts to be closed at once, and that, in an uncertain world, never happens.

That is why the Fortress does not rest even when everything is fine, because its work is not to respond to a present danger, it is to watch all possible dangers, and that watch has no end, it consumes it from within without there being, on the outside, any threat to justify so much wear.
The Gallery of Masks

In practical life, the Fortress is the calendar with no free days, the house where each thing has its exact place and where disorder provokes an anxiety that far exceeds the disorder, the trip planned to the minute that leaves no room for chance, where the best of a trip usually dwells.
We call these people organised, and they are, but organisation, when it is Fortress, has stopped being a tool at the service of life and become a cage in which less and less of life fits.
In the head, the Fortress produces a mask that has become epidemic: the thirst to know now. You cannot bear an open question, a matter without an answer, a doubt left hanging.

You need to resolve, to close, to know now, and if you do not know, you search, you research, you ask, until you obtain an answer, whatever it may be, because the uncertainty of the open question is more unbearable than a hasty answer, even a wrong one.

We live surrounded by instruments that feed this hunger, that promise an immediate answer to everything, and the Fortress clings to them, consults without stopping, informs itself compulsively, in the illusion that more information will finally put it safe from not knowing. It does not.

Most of life's questions do not close with information, they stay open because it is in their nature to stay, and the thirst to know now, by demanding an immediate closure, either contents itself with false answers that quiet it for an instant, or circles endlessly in search of a certainty that does not come.

Learning to leave a question open, to live with a doubt without rushing to close it, to bear not knowing without filling it with hasty answers, is one of the deepest disciplines against this face, because it is the exact opposite of what it asks, and it is where the patience it never had resides.

And there is a mask of the Fortress within thought itself, subtle and dangerous: black-and-white. Because nuance is uncertain, because the maybe is uncomfortable, because grey gives no firm ground to set the foot, the Fortress prefers simple certainties to complex truths.
It divides the world into good and bad, right and wrong, ours and theirs, because that sharp division gives a safety that reality, full of shades, denies.

It is easier to have a firm and closed opinion than to sustain the uncertainty of a question that has reason on several sides, and so the Fortress clings to absolute positions, to certainties that let no doubt in, not because they are true, but because they are firm, and firmness soothes the alarm better than truth.

Notice how this impoverishes thought and hardens the heart: a person who needs simple certainties becomes incapable of understanding the complexity of others, of changing her mind, of bearing the discomfort of not having a closed opinion about everything.
